title: Finger Millet (Rapoko/Rukweza) Production Guide for Zimbabwe
content:
FINGER MILLET (RAPOKO/RUKWEZA) PRODUCTION IN ZIMBABWE

Finger millet (Eleusine coracana) is one of Zimbabwe's most important traditional cereals, known as 'rapoko' or 'rukweza' in Shona. It is deeply embedded in cultural practices and is critical for food security in semi-arid regions.

IMPORTANCE:
- Extremely nutritious: 3x more calcium than other cereals, rich in iron and methionine
- Gluten-free and diabetic-friendly (slow-release carbohydrates)
- Stores for 2-5 years without significant pest damage (naturally resistant to storage pests)
- Used for porridge (bota), traditional beer (hwahwa/kachasu), flour, and malting
- Cultural significance: used in ancestral ceremonies and social gatherings

VARIETIES:
- ICIAP-SM 1 (ICRISAT): Improved variety, good blast resistance, 100 days, yields 1.0-2.5 t/ha
- FMV 1 (DR&SS): Locally improved, adapted to communal farming, 110 days, yields 0.8-2.0 t/ha
- Local landraces: Lower yielding but culturally preferred. OPV - farmers retain seed.

SOIL AND CLIMATE:
- Tolerates poor, sandy soils that are unsuitable for maize
- pH 5.0-7.0 (very adaptable)
- Rainfall: 350-600mm (ideal for Natural Regions III, IV, V)
- Altitude: up to 2400m

LAND PREPARATION:
- Plough early (September-October) to bury weeds
- Harrow to very fine tilth (finger millet seed is tiny, 1-2mm)
- A firm, level seedbed is essential for uniform germination

PLANTING:
- Plant November to mid-December with first effective rains
- Seed rate: 5-8 kg/ha (very small seed)
- CRITICAL: Mix seed with dry sand (1:3 ratio) for even distribution
- Planting depth: 1-2cm MAXIMUM (surface sow and press/roll into soil)
- Row spacing: 30cm
- Thin to 10-15cm between plants after emergence
- Can also broadcast and incorporate with light raking

FERTILIZATION:
- Basal: Compound D at 100-150 kg/ha (or well-rotted manure at 5-10 t/ha)
- Top dress: AN at 50-100 kg/ha at tillering (3-4 weeks after emergence)
- Responds well to manure and compost

WEEDING:
- CRITICAL limiting factor for finger millet production
- 2-3 weedings essential. Finger millet is a poor competitor in early growth.
- First weeding at 2-3 weeks (thin simultaneously)
- Second weeding at 5-6 weeks
- Late weeding renders crop almost worthless

PEST AND DISEASE MANAGEMENT:
- Quelea birds: MAJOR pest at grain filling. Bird scaring from heading to maturity is essential. Plant near homestead. Coordinate with community.
- Shoot Fly: Attacks seedlings. Early planting helps avoid peak populations.
- Blast (Magnaporthe): Most important disease. Use resistant varieties (ICIAP-SM 1). Avoid late planting.
- Smut: Use clean seed. Remove and destroy infected heads before spores spread.

HARVESTING:
- Cut heads when grain is hard and fingers close/curl inward
- Dry heads on clean surface for 1-2 weeks
- Thresh by beating dried heads. Winnow.
- Yield: 0.8-2.5 t/ha depending on variety and management

POST-HARVEST:
- Excellent storage: 2-5 years if properly dried (12% moisture)
- Naturally resistant to most storage pests due to tiny grain size
- Store in granaries, clay pots, or bags
- No chemical treatment needed for storage (unlike maize)

title: Pearl Millet (Mhunga) Production Guide for Zimbabwe
content:
PEARL MILLET (MHUNGA) PRODUCTION IN ZIMBABWE

Pearl millet (Pennisetum glaucum) is the most drought-tolerant cereal crop. Known as 'mhunga' in Shona, it is essential for food security in Zimbabwe's driest regions (Natural Regions IV and V) where maize consistently fails.

WHY PEARL MILLET IN DRY REGIONS:
- Survives and produces grain on as little as 250mm rainfall
- Can recover from drought stress better than any other cereal
- Produces food even in seasons when maize fails completely
- Higher energy content than maize
- Rich in iron, zinc, protein, and B vitamins
- Gluten-free

VARIETIES:
- Okashana 1 (ICRISAT/SADC): Early maturing (85 days), excellent drought escape, yields 1.5-3.0 t/ha
- PMV 2 (DR&SS): Locally adapted, good tillering, 90 days, yields 1.0-2.5 t/ha
- Local landraces: OPV - farmers can retain seed. Lower yielding but adapted.

SOIL AND CLIMATE:
- Tolerates the poorest, sandiest, most infertile soils
- pH 5.0-8.0 (extremely adaptable)
- Rainfall: 250-500mm
- Most heat-tolerant cereal - thrives where temperatures exceed 35C

PLANTING:
- Plant November to December with first effective rains
- Seed rate: 3-5 kg/ha
- Row spacing: 75cm, in-row spacing: 15-20cm
- Planting depth: 2-3cm
- Can be planted in rows or in planting stations (3-4 seeds per station, thin to 2)

FERTILIZATION:
- Basal: Compound D at 100-200 kg/ha (or manure at 5-10 t/ha)
- Top dress: AN at 50-100 kg/ha at 3-4 weeks IF rains are adequate
- In drought years, reduce or skip top-dressing

WEEDING:
- 2 weedings critical. Pearl millet is slow to establish.
- First weeding at 2-3 weeks. Thin simultaneously.
- Second weeding at 5-6 weeks.

PEST MANAGEMENT:
- Quelea birds: MAJOR threat from heading onwards. Bird scaring essential. Coordinate with neighbours.
- Stalk Borer: Apply Carbaryl granules into leaf whorl if severe.
- Downy Mildew: Use clean seed. Remove infected plants.

HARVESTING:
- Cut heads when grain is hard
- Dry for 1-2 weeks on clean surface
- Thresh and winnow

POST-HARVEST:
- Dry to <12% moisture
- More susceptible to storage pests than finger millet
- Treat with Actellic Super for stored grain protection
- Store in airtight containers or PICS bags

USES:
- Sadza/stiff porridge (mahewu)
- Thin porridge
- Traditional beer
- Flour for baking
- Livestock feed (whole plant)

title: Cowpea (Nyemba) Production Guide for Zimbabwe
content:
COWPEA (NYEMBA) PRODUCTION IN ZIMBABWE

Cowpea (Vigna unguiculata), known as 'nyemba' in Shona, is a vital dual-purpose legume in Zimbabwe - both grain and leaves are consumed. It is the most important food legume for smallholder farmers in dry regions.

NUTRITIONAL VALUE:
- Grain: 23-25% protein, rich in iron, zinc, and folate
- Leaves: Excellent source of Vitamin A, iron, protein, and calcium
- Both grain and leaves are consumed, making it a 'complete' crop
- Provides essential amino acid lysine that is deficient in cereal-based diets

VARIETIES:
- CBC 1 (DR&SS): Early (75 days), cream grain, erect habit, yields 1.0-2.0 t/ha
- CBC 2 / Nyemba (DR&SS): Medium (85 days), spreading habit, good for intercropping
- IT 18 (IITA): Extra-early (65 days), white grain (premium market), erect, yields 1.2-2.5 t/ha

PLANTING:
- Sole crop: November to January
- Intercrop: December to January (after maize established)
- Row spacing: 60-75cm, in-row: 15-20cm
- Seed rate: 20-30 kg/ha
- Planting depth: 3-5cm

INTERCROPPING:
- Traditionally intercropped with maize, sorghum, or millet
- Plant between cereal rows after cereal is knee-high
- Fixes 40-80 kg nitrogen/ha - benefits subsequent cereal crop
- Excellent preceding crop for maize

FERTILIZATION:
- Minimal fertilizer needed - cowpea fixes its own nitrogen
- Basal: Compound D at 100-150 kg/ha (for phosphorus)
- DO NOT apply nitrogen fertilizer (inhibits nodulation)
- Inoculate with Bradyrhizobium on new fields

LEAF HARVESTING:
- Young leaves and tender tips harvested as vegetable (munyemba) from 4-6 weeks
- Pick tender growing tips, leaving enough leaf area for grain production
- Leaves cooked as relish, dried for later use
- Regular tip-picking encourages branching

PEST MANAGEMENT:
- Bean Fly: Seed dress with Imidacloprid. Most damaging in first 3 weeks.
- Pod Borer (Maruca vitrata): Scout from flowering. Spray Cypermethrin if threshold reached.
- Flower Thrips: Spray at flowering if severe.
- Blister Beetle: Hand-pick (wear gloves - they cause blisters).
- Storage Bruchids (Callosobruchus): MAJOR post-harvest pest. Treat with Actellic or use PICS (Purdue Improved Crop Storage) bags.

HARVESTING:
- Harvest pods when dry and brown
- Multiple pickings as pods mature unevenly (especially spreading types)
- Dry pods, thresh, winnow

POST-HARVEST STORAGE:
- CRITICAL: Cowpea bruchids can destroy entire harvest within 3-4 months
- Triple-bagging (PICS bags): Seal grain in 3 nested polyethylene bags. Suffocates bruchids.
- Chemical: Treat with Actellic Super dust
- Hermetic storage: Metal silos or jerrycans with tight lids
- Traditional: Mix with ash or hot pepper (partial protection only)

title: Bambara Nut (Nyimo) Production Guide for Zimbabwe
content:
BAMBARA NUT (NYIMO) PRODUCTION IN ZIMBABWE

Bambara groundnut (Vigna subterranea), known as 'nyimo' in Shona, is one of Africa's most important indigenous crops. It produces pods underground (like groundnuts) and is extremely drought tolerant.

CULTURAL SIGNIFICANCE:
- Traditionally a 'women's crop' in Zimbabwe
- Used in cultural ceremonies and social gatherings
- Growing commercial interest as a nutritious, climate-resilient crop
- Sometimes called 'the crop that can save Africa' due to its resilience

NUTRITIONAL VALUE:
- Complete food: 18-24% protein, 50-60% carbohydrates, 6-12% fat
- Contains lysine (limiting amino acid in cereal diets)
- Rich in iron, potassium, and B vitamins
- Higher energy density than most legumes

VARIETIES:
- Local Red: Traditional landrace, red-seeded, 130 days, yields 0.5-1.5 t/ha
- Local Cream: Earlier (120 days), preferred for flour/commercial, yields 0.6-1.8 t/ha
- No major breeding programmes yet - mostly landraces

PLANTING:
- Plant November to December with first rains
- Row spacing: 45cm, in-row: 15-20cm
- Seed rate: 50-80 kg/ha
- Planting depth: 3-5cm
- IMPORTANT: Use SANDY, light soils. Bambara produces very poorly in heavy clays.

FERTILIZATION:
- Minimal or none required. Fixes its own nitrogen.
- Compound D at 50-100 kg/ha if available (for phosphorus)
- Responds well to manure/compost
- Low-input crop ideal for resource-poor farmers

WATER:
- Extremely drought tolerant - produces food on 300-500mm rainfall
- Produces food in years when maize and even groundnuts fail
- Critical food security crop for dry regions

WEEDING:
- 2-3 weedings. First at 3 weeks, second at 6 weeks.
- After canopy closure, crop suppresses weeds.

PEST AND DISEASE:
- Generally pest/disease free (a major advantage)
- Termites: Can damage pods underground. Apply Chlorpyrifos in termite-prone fields.
- Cercospora Leaf Spot: Minor. No treatment usually needed.
- Root rot: In waterlogged soils only. Avoid heavy clays.

HARVESTING:
- Uproot entire plant when leaves yellow and begin drying (5-6 months)
- Dry whole plants for 1-2 weeks
- Remove pods by hand
- Shell, dry to <10% moisture

POST-HARVEST:
- Very good storage life (naturally resistant to storage pests)
- Store dried seed in airtight containers
- Can store for 1-2 years if dry

USES:
- Mutakura: Boiled fresh bambara nuts (popular snack)
- Dried and ground into flour for fortifying porridge
- Roasted as snack
- Hummus-like paste
- Livestock feed

CLIMATE RESILIENCE:
- Bambara nut is one of the most climate-resilient crops available
- Produces food on marginal land where other crops fail
- Should be promoted more widely as part of Zimbabwe's climate adaptation strategy

title: Butternut and Pumpkin Production Guide for Zimbabwe
content:
BUTTERNUT AND PUMPKIN PRODUCTION IN ZIMBABWE

Butternut squash (Cucurbita moschata) is one of Zimbabwe's most popular vegetables at urban and rural markets. It is an excellent source of Vitamin A (beta-carotene) and stores for months without refrigeration.

VARIETIES:
- Waltham (OP): Standard variety, bell-shaped, 1.5-3.0 kg, 100 days, yields 15-30 t/ha
- Star 6001 (Starke Ayres): High-yielding hybrid, uniform shape, 90 days, yields 20-40 t/ha
- Local pumpkin varieties: Flat, ribbed pumpkins also widely grown for leaves and fruit

SOIL AND CLIMATE:
- Fertile, well-drained loams to clay loams
- pH 5.5-6.8
- Warm-season crop: needs soil temperature >18C
- Rainfall: 500-800mm or irrigation

PLANTING:
- Direct seed October to December
- Can also transplant seedlings (sow in trays 2-3 weeks before transplanting)
- Spacing: 200cm between rows, 60-80cm between plants (butternuts need space to run)
- Planting depth: 3-5cm, 2-3 seeds per station, thin to 1
- Seed rate: 3-4 kg/ha

FERTILIZATION:
- Heavy feeder, responds well to organic matter
- Basal: Compound D at 300-400 kg/ha OR well-rotted manure at 10-20 t/ha
- Top dress: LAN at 100 kg/ha when first runners appear
- Side-dress with potassium for fruit development

IRRIGATION:
- 25-30mm per week
- Drip irrigation ideal (keeps leaves dry, reduces disease)
- Critical at flowering and fruit development
- Reduce irrigation as fruit matures for better storage quality

PEST AND DISEASE:
- Fruit Fly (MAJOR): Use protein bait traps (GF-120). Remove fallen fruit. Spray Malathion as last resort.
- Aphids: Transmit Mosaic Virus. Control with Dimethoate/Imidacloprid.
- Powdery Mildew: Common in late season. Spray Sulphur or Triadimefon.
- Downy Mildew: Spray Mancozeb/Metalaxyl.
- Mosaic Virus: Remove infected plants. Control aphid vectors.

HARVESTING:
- Harvest when stem dries and skin is hard (cannot dent with thumbnail)
- Leave 5cm stem attached (prevents rot entry)
- Handle carefully to avoid bruising

POST-HARVEST:
- EXCELLENT storage crop: 3-6 months at ambient temperature
- Cure in sun for 7-10 days to harden skin
- Store in cool, dry, ventilated area
- Check stored fruit monthly and remove any showing rot
- Peak market prices in dry season (August-October) when supply is low

PUMPKIN LEAVES:
- Pumpkin leaves (muboora/nhopi) are an important vegetable in Zimbabwe
- Harvested from trailing pumpkin plants as a pot herb
- Rich in Vitamin A, iron, and protein
- Can be dried for later use

title: Paprika and Chilli Production Guide for Zimbabwe
content:
PAPRIKA AND CHILLI PRODUCTION IN ZIMBABWE

Paprika (Capsicum annuum) is a high-value cash crop in Zimbabwe, grown primarily for export. Zimbabwe is a significant producer of paprika oleoresin used in the food colouring, cosmetics, and pharmaceutical industries. It offers smallholder farmers a high return per hectare compared to traditional crops.

MARKET AND ECONOMICS:
- Paprika contracts available through Paprika International and other registered buyers
- Price determined by ASTA colour value (higher colour = higher price)
- Target ASTA >180 for premium prices
- Gross margins of USD 2,000-5,000 per hectare (far exceeding maize)
- Growing global demand for natural food colourants

VARIETIES:
- PRI Paprika: Standard variety, ASTA >180, 150 days, yields 1.5-3.5 t/ha dried
- Serrano Hot Chilli: Hot type, 10000-25000 SHU, 120 days, yields 2.0-5.0 t/ha fresh

NURSERY:
- Sow in seedbeds/trays July-August (6-8 weeks before transplanting)
- Use sterilised growing media
- Harden seedlings for 1 week before transplanting
- Seedlings ready at 6-8 leaf stage

TRANSPLANTING:
- September to November
- Row spacing: 90cm, in-row: 30-40cm
- Irrigate immediately after transplanting
- Mulch to conserve moisture and suppress weeds

FERTILIZATION:
- Basal: Compound S at 400-600 kg/ha at transplanting
- Top dress: AN at 100-150 kg/ha every 4 weeks (3-4 applications)
- Potassium important for colour development
- Fertigation through drip ideal

IRRIGATION:
- Regular, consistent irrigation essential
- 25-30mm per week
- Drip irrigation highly recommended (reduces disease, saves water)
- Water stress causes flower and fruit drop

PEST AND DISEASE:
- Aphids: Transmit viruses (Mosaic, TYLCV). Control early with Imidacloprid.
- Fruit Fly: Protein bait traps, sanitation.
- Bollworm: Scout from flowering, spray Emamectin benzoate.
- Bacterial Spot: Copper-based sprays preventatively.
- Phytophthora Root Rot: Improve drainage, avoid overwatering.
- Mosaic Virus: Remove infected plants, control aphids, use resistant varieties.

HARVESTING:
- Multiple harvests over 3-4 months
- Pick when pods are fully red and mature
- Do not pick green or partially red pods (low ASTA)
- Pick every 10-14 days

DRYING (Critical for Quality):
- Sun dry on raised platforms/drying racks (not on bare ground)
- Spread pods in single layer
- Turn daily for even drying
- Takes 7-14 days depending on weather
- Pods must snap cleanly when dry (target moisture <11%)
- Mechanical dryers available for larger operations
- NEVER dry on tar/asphalt roads (contaminants reduce quality)

GRADING AND MARKETING:
- Grade by colour intensity, cleanliness, moisture content
- Pack in clean bags (not fertiliser bags)
- Store in cool, dry, dark place (light degrades colour)
- Sell to registered buyers (Paprika International, etc.)
- Maintain traceability records
